name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّـٰكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَـٰيِشَ ۗ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
Certainly We have established you on the earth, and made in it [various] means of livelihood for you. Little do you thank. (10)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَـٰكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ لَمْ يَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (١١)
```

```english
Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam.’ So they [all] prostrated, but not Iblis: he was not among those who prostrated. (11)
```

```arabic
قَالَ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ ۖ قَالَ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ (١٢)
```

```english
Said He, ‘What prevented you from prostrating, when I commanded you? ’‘I am better than him,’ he said. ‘You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’ (12)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَٱهْبِطْ مِنْهَا فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَن تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا فَٱخْرُجْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
‘Get down from it!’ He said. ‘It is not for you to be arrogant therein. Begone! You are indeed among the degraded ones.’ (13)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘Respite me till the day they will be resurrected.’ (14)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ (١٥)
```

```english
Said He, ‘You are indeed among the reprieved.’ (15)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَبِمَآ أَغْوَيْتَنِى لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَٰطَكَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (١٦)
```

```english
‘As You have consigned me to perversity,’ he said, ‘I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path. (16)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ لَـَٔاتِيَنَّهُم مِّنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَعَنْ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ وَعَن شَمَآئِلِهِمْ ۖ وَلَا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شَـٰكِرِينَ (١٧)
```

```english
Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear, and from their right and their left, and You will not find most of them to be grateful.’ (17)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا مَذْءُومًا مَّدْحُورًا ۖ لَّمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٨)
```

```english
Said He, ‘Begone hence, blameful and banished! Whoever of them follows you, I will surely fill hell with you all.’ (18)
```

```arabic
وَيَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ فَكُلَا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٩)
```

```english
[Then He said to Adam,] ‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in paradise, and eat thereof whence you wish; but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers.’ (19)
```

```arabic
فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِيُبْدِىَ لَهُمَا مَا وُۥرِىَ عَنْهُمَا مِن سَوْءَٰتِهِمَا وَقَالَ مَا نَهَىٰكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِدِينَ (٢٠)
```

```english
Then Satan tempted them, to expose to them what was hidden from them of their nakedness, and he said, ‘Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you become immortal.’ (20)
```

```arabic
وَقَاسَمَهُمَآ إِنِّى لَكُمَا لَمِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ (٢١)
```

```english
And he swore to them, ‘I am indeed your well-wisher.’ (21)
```

```arabic
فَدَلَّىٰهُمَا بِغُرُورٍ ۚ فَلَمَّا ذَاقَا ٱلشَّجَرَةَ بَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ وَنَادَىٰهُمَا رَبُّهُمَآ أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا ٱلشَّجَرَةِ وَأَقُل لَّكُمَآ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لَكُمَا عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٢٢)
```

```english
Thus he brought about their fall by deception. So when they tasted of the tree, their nakedness became exposed to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree, and tell you, ‘‘Satan is indeed your manifest enemy?’’ ’ (22)
```

```arabic
قَالَا رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَآ أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٢٣)
```

```english
They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers.’ (23)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٢٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.’ (24)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘In it you will live, and in it you will die; and from it you will be raised [from the dead].’ (25)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses describe the beginning of the creation of man and his fashioning, and what occurred therein regarding the command to the angels to prostrate to him, their prostration, the refusal of Iblis, his deception of Adam and his wife, and their exit from the Garden—along with the decree Allah ordained in that regard.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَايِشَ قَلِيلًا مَا تَشْكُرُونَ — **"Certainly We have established you on the earth and made in it [the means of] your livelihood. Little do you give thanks."** (7:10). "Establishment (التمكين) on the earth" refers to providing habitation and settlement therein; that is, We made the earth your place (مكان). It is also possible that "establishment" carries the meaning of granting power (الإقدار) and dominion (التسليط). This second meaning is supported by the fact that these verses parallel, in a sense, what is mentioned in *Surah al-Baqarah* regarding the story of Adam and Iblis, where the verses began with His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مَا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا — **"It is He who created for you all that is in the earth"** (2:29), which refers to dominion and subjugation.

However, since the current verses conclude with His saying: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَى حِينٍ — **"and for you there is an abode on the earth and a provision for a while"** (7:24), the first meaning is more appropriate. His saying: وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ (etc.) serves as a summary of what the subsequent verses detail until the end of the story of the Garden.

"Livelihoods (المعايش)" is the plural of *ma'ishah* (معيشة), which refers to that by which one lives, such as food, drink, and the like. The verse is in the context of reminding them of His favors—the blessing of inhabiting the earth or having dominion and mastery over it, and providing for them therein various means of life. For this reason, the speech concludes with His saying: قَلِيلًا مَا تَشْكُرُونَ — **"Little do you give thanks."**

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ — **"Certainly We created you, then We fashioned you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam.’"** (7:11).

The story begins from this verse and continues for fifteen verses, detailing the summary of the previous verse and clarifying the causes and reasons that led to the "establishment of man on the earth" indicated by His saying: وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَايِشَ.

Therefore, the speech in His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ (etc.) begins with the *lam* of the oath (لام القسم). For this reason also, the two stories—the command to prostrate and the story of the Garden—are presented as a single narrative without separating the second story by anything indicating it is an independent account. All of this is to transition toward His saying: قَالَ اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ — **"He said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode’"** (7:24) until the end of the two verses, so that the detail corresponds to the summary of His saying: وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ.

Regarding His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَاكُمْ — **"Certainly We created you, then We fashioned you"**, the address is to all human beings. It is an address of favor (امتناني), as seen in the previous verse, because the content is the same, differing only in brevity and detail.

Accordingly, the transition in the address from the general to the specific—namely His saying: ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ — **"then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’"** after His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَاكُمْ — serves to clarify two truths:

First: The prostration of the angels was for all the children of Adam—that is, for the human species (النشأة الإنسانية)—even though Adam (peace be upon him) was the designated direction (القبلة) for the prostration. In the matter of prostration, he (peace be upon him) was a prototype representing humanity, standing in for the multitude of human individuals, rather than being the object of prostration solely for his individual person. This is similar to the *Ka'bah* being made a direction (قبلة) toward which one turns in worship, representing the side of Lordship (الربوبية).

This meaning is derived, firstly, from the story of the vicegerency (الخلافة) mentioned in *Surah al-Baqarah* (2:30-33). What is understood from the verses there is that the command to the angels to prostrate is a branch of the vicegerency. The vicegerency mentioned in those verses—as we inferred there—is not exclusive to Adam but applies to all human beings; thus, the prostration is also for all.

Secondly: Iblis targeted them—meaning the children of Adam—directly, without Adam being an intermediary or being exclusively targeted, when he said, as narrated by Allah, the Glorified: فَبِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَاطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ ثُمَّ لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ — **"As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path. Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear"** (7:16-17), without any prior mention of the children of Adam. A similar instance occurs in *Surah al-Hijr* where he said: رَبِّ بِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — **"My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them, all together"** (15:39), and in *Surah Sad*: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — **"By Your might, I will surely pervert them, all together"** (38:82). Had they not all been objects of prostration for the angels by virtue of their species, it would not have been consistent for him to seek this vengeance against them from the outset, as is evident.

Thirdly: The addresses that Allah, the Glorified, directed to Adam (peace be upon him)—as in *Surah al-Baqarah* and *Surah Taha*—He generalized in this very Surah to all his children, saying: يَا بَنِي آدَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ — **"O Children of Adam! If there come to you messengers from among you"** (7:35), etc.

The second truth: The creation of Adam (peace be upon him) was a creation for all, as indicated by His saying: وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسَانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلَالَةٍ مِنْ مَاءٍ مَهِينٍ — **"and He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid"** (32:7-8), and His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ — **"It is He who created you from dust, then from a drop, then from a clinging mass"** (40:67). The apparent meaning of these two verses is that the "creation from dust" refers to what occurred in Adam (peace be upon him).

This is also suggested by the words of Iblis within the story, as narrated by Allah, the Glorified, in *Surah al-Isra'*: لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا — **"If You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, [all] but a few"** (17:62). It is also hinted at by His saying: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَى أَنْفُسِهِمْ — **"When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness about themselves"** (7:172), as will be explained later.

The exegetes have various opinions regarding this verse. It is stated in *Majma' al-Bayan*: "Then the Glorified mentioned His favor in the beginning of creation, saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَاكُمْ. Al-Akhfash said: 'Then (ثُمَّ)' here carries the meaning of 'and (الواو)'. Az-Zajjaj said: 'This is an error that al-Khalil, Sibawayh, and all those whose knowledge is trusted would not permit. Rather, "then" is only for that which occurs after what was mentioned before it, and nothing else. The meaning in this address is the mention of the beginning of creation first; thus, the intent is: We began the creation of Adam, then We fashioned him. The creation of Adam began from dust, then the fashioning occurred afterward. This is the meaning of "We created you, then We fashioned you," and then "We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’" after the completion of Adam's creation.' This is narrated from al-Hasan. It is part of the speech of the Arabs to say: 'We did such and such to you,' while they mean their ancestors. In the Revelation: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ الطُّورَ — **"And when We took a pledge from you, and raised the Mount above you"** (2:63), meaning the pledge of your ancestors."

Other opinions have been mentioned: Among them is that the meaning is: "We created Adam, then We fashioned you in his loins, then We said to the angels, 'Prostrate to Adam,'" narrated from Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, ar-Rabi', Qatadah, and as-Suddi.

Another is that the sequence (الترتيب) lies in the reporting (الإخبار); as if He said: "We created you, then We fashioned you, then We inform you that We said to the angels, 'Prostrate to Adam,'" just as one says: "I am walking, then I am hurrying." This is the view of a group of grammarians, including Ali ibn Isa, al-Qadi Abu Sa'id al-Sirafi, and others. Accordingly, it has been said that the meaning is: "We created you in the loins of men, then We fashioned you in the wombs of women," narrated from Ikrimah. It was also said: "We created you in the womb, then We fashioned you by splitting the hearing, sight, and other limbs." (End of quote).

As for the view narrated from az-Zajjaj, it is problematic. Firstly, attributing the attributes or actions of predecessors to their descendants is only valid if the two groups share in that matter through some form of participation (اشتراك)—as in the example he provided—not merely by the relation of lineage, precedence, and succession. It is not valid, by mere genealogical affiliation, to consider the creation of Adam's person as the creation of his children unless his creation was their creation in some way.

Secondly, if what he mentioned were correct—that the creation and fashioning of Adam could be counted as the creation and fashioning of his children—then it would also be correct to count the command to the angels to prostrate to him as a command to prostrate to his children, as occurred in His saying: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ الطُّورَ. Why then did He say: ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ — **"then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’"** and not say: "then We said to the angels, 'Prostrate before man'"?

As for the opinions he narrated last, they are weak views not understood from the wording of the verse. Perhaps those who held them would not accept such interpretations for their own speech, so how can the most eloquent of speech be interpreted in such a manner?

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ لَمْ يَكُنْ مِنَ السَّاجِدِينَ — **"So they prostrated, but not Iblis: he was not one of those who prostrate."** (7:11). The Exalted informed of the prostration of all the angels, as He explicitly states in His saying: فَسَجَدَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ — **"Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together"** (15:30), and He excepted Iblis from them. He explained the reason for his failure to comply with the command in another place, saying: كَانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ — **"He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed the command of his Lord"** (18:50). He described the angels with words such as: بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — **"Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command"** (21:26-27). This outwardly indicates that he was of a different species than the angels.

For this reason, a disagreement occurred among them in justifying this exception: Is it a connected exception (استثناء متصل) by way of the angels being the majority and more noble, or is it a disconnected exception (استثناء منفصل), and he was commanded by a separate command other than the one directed to the assembly of angels? This is despite the apparent meaning of His saying: مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ — **"What prevented you from prostrating, when I commanded you?"** (7:12), which suggests the command was but one, directed by Allah to the angels.

What is understood from the apparent speech of the Exalted is that Iblis was with the angels without being distinguished from them. The station (المقام) that gathered them all was the station of sanctity (مقام القدس), as inferred from the story of the vicegerency: وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً قَالُوا أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ — **"When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’"** (2:30). The command to prostrate was directed to that station—meaning to those residing in that station by virtue of their station—as indicated by His saying later: قَالَ فَاهْبِطْ مِنْهَا فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَنْ تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا — **"He said, ‘Get down from it! It is not for you to be arrogant here’"** (7:13). The pronoun refers to the rank (المنزلة), the heaven, or the Garden, and they all lead back to the rank and station. Had the address been directed to them without the rank and station playing a role, it would have been appropriate to say: "It is not for you to be arrogant."

Accordingly, there was no difference between him and the angels before that. At that point, the two groups became distinct. The angels remained in what their station and rank required—which is servile humility (الخضوع العبودي) and compliance—as Allah narrated of them: بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ. This is the reality of the life of the angels and the nature of their acts. They remained upon that, while Iblis exited the rank he had shared with them, as indicated by His saying: كَانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ. Transgression (الفسق) is the exiting of a date from its skin. Thus, he became distinct from them and took up a life that has no reality except exiting from divine honor and the obedience of servitude.

Although the story is presented in the manner of social narratives familiar to us—containing a command, compliance, rebellion, argumentation, expulsion, stoning, and other legislative and master-servant matters—the preceding explanation derived from the verses guides us to the fact that it is a representation (تمثيل) of the creative process (التكوين). This means that Iblis, in the state he was in, did not accept compliance—that is, humility toward the human reality—and thus disobedience branched out from him. This is suggested by His saying: فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَنْ تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا, for its apparent meaning is that this station does not, by its very nature, accept arrogance; thus, his arrogance within it was his exit from it and his descent to what is below it.

Furthermore, the command to prostrate—as you have known—was a single command directed to the angels and Iblis together. A command directed to the angels is not of the nature of a legislative command (مولويا تشريعيا)—meaning a command related to an act where the subject's relation to obedience and disobedience, or felicity and wretchedness, is equal. For the angels are fashioned (مجبولون) for obedience and established in the abode of felicity, just as Iblis fell into the opposite side of that, as became apparent from his state when the command was directed to him.

Had Allah, the Glorified, not created Adam and commanded the angels and Iblis together to prostrate to him, Iblis would have remained in his station of proximity, indistinguishable from the angels. But the creation of man split the station into two: a station of proximity and a station of distance; and it distinguished the path into two: the path of felicity and the path of wretchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ قَالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نَارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ — **"He said, ‘What prevented you from prostrating, when I commanded you?’ He said, ‘I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’"** (7:12). He means: "What prevented you from prostrating," as occurs in *Surah Sad*: قَالَ يَا إِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ — **"He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands?’"** (38:75). For this reason, it is sometimes said that "not (لَا)" is extra, brought for emphasis, as in His saying: لِئَلَّا يَعْلَمَ أَهْلُ الْكِتَابِ أَلَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَى شَيْءٍ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللَّهِ — **"so that the People of the Book may know that they do not have any power over any of Allah’s grace"** (57:29).

The apparent meaning is that "prevented (مَنَعَ)" implies a meaning similar to "compelled" or "called upon." The meaning is: "What compelled you or called upon you not to prostrate, acting as a preventative for you?"

His saying: قَالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نَارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ — narrates the answer he gave, may he be cursed. This was his first disobedience and the first disobedience by which Allah, the Glorified, was defied. All disobediences, upon analysis, return to the claim of "I-ness" (الإنية) and contending with Allah, the Glorified, in His pride (كبريائه). To Him belongs the cloak of pride, and He has no partner in it. It is not for a created servant to rely on his own essence and say "I" in the face of the Divine "I-ness" before which existence has humbled itself, necks have bowed, voices have been hushed, and everything has become abased.

Had his soul not been drawn to itself, and had his gaze not been confined to witnessing his own "I-ness," he would not have been restricted by the independence of his essence. He would have witnessed the Self-Subsistent (القيوم) Deity above him, and his "I-ness" would have been abased before Him—an abasement that negates all independence and pride. He would then have submitted to the Divine command, and his soul would have complied in obedience. His soul would not have been drawn to what appeared to be his being "better than him" because he is from fire and the other is from clay; rather, his soul would have been drawn to the command issuing from the source of Greatness and Pride, the fountainhead of all Beauty and Majesty.

It would have been appropriate, upon hearing His saying: مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ, to provide a matching answer, such as saying: "What prevented me is that I am better than him." However, he said: أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ — **"I am better than him"** — to manifest the "I-ness" and to imply stability and continuity. It is also understood from this that what prevented him from prostrating was the "betterness" he saw for himself. Thus, his saying "I am better than him" is more manifest and emphatic in conveying arrogance.

From here it becomes clear that this arrogance was arrogance against Allah, the Glorified, rather than just arrogance against Adam.

Furthermore, in his saying: أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نَارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ, he argued for his being better than Adam by the origin of his creation, which is fire, and that it is better than the clay from which Adam was created. Allah, the Glorified, confirmed what he mentioned regarding the origin of his creation when He mentioned that he was of the jinn, and that the jinn were created from fire. The Exalted said: كَانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ — **"He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed the command of his Lord"** (18:50), and said: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ مِنْ صَلْصَالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْنَاهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ مِنْ نَارِ السَّمُومِ — **"Certainly We created man from a dry clay [drawn] from an altered mud. And the jinn We created before from a smokeless fire"** (15:26-27), and also said: خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ مِنْ صَلْصَالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ وَخَلَقَ الْجَانَّ مِنْ مَارِجٍ مِنْ نَارٍ — **"He created man from dry clay like pottery, and He created the jinn from a flame of fire"** (55:14-15).

However, He did not confirm his claim of being better than him. Although He did not refute his saying "I am better than him, You created me from fire..." etc., in this Surah, He clarified the excellence of Adam over him and over the angels in the discourse on vicegerency (الخلافة) which He mentioned to the angels in *Surah al-Baqarah*.

Moreover, the Exalted mentioned the story in another place, saying: إِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي خَالِقٌ بَشَرًا مِنْ طِينٍ فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ سَاجِدِينَ فَسَجَدَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ اسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ قَالَ يَا إِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْعَالِينَ قَالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نَارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ — **"When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human being out of clay. So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration.’ Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together, but not Iblis: he acted arrogantly and he was one of the faithless. He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands? Were you arrogant, or are you among the exalted?’ He said, ‘I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’"** (38:71-76).

Thus, He clarified, firstly, that they were not called to prostrate to him because of his earthly matter from which he was proportioned; rather, they were called to that because of His proportioning him and breathing into him of His spirit—which is attributed specifically to the Exalted, carrying all honor, and being the object of complete Divine care. The matter of "betterness" in creative realities (التكوينيات) revolves around Divine care, not by a judgment from their own essences, for there is no judgment except for Allah.

Then He clarified, secondly, when He asked him about the reason for his failure to prostrate by saying: مَا مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ, that the Exalted took the utmost interest in the matter of his creation and gave it every care, as He created him with both His hands—by whatever meaning "the two hands" is interpreted. This is the excellence. But the cursed one replied by saying: أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نَارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ, clinging to the matter of fire and clay, and ignoring the matter of his arrogance against his Lord. Similarly, in this Surah, he was asked about the reason for his arrogance against his Lord when it was said to him: مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ, yet he clung to his saying: "I am better than him..." etc., and paid no heed to what he was asked—namely, the reason for his arrogance against his Lord by not complying with His command.

Indeed, he did pay heed to it when he said: "I am better than him," for he affirmed for himself an independent "I-ness" in the face of the Divine "I-ness" that has subdued everything. This led him to forget His Greatness, the Exalted, and he found himself like his Lord, possessing independence like His independence. This necessitated his neglecting the obligation of complying with His command because He is Allah; instead, he occupied himself with "preponderating factors" (المرجحات) and found the preponderance of disobedience over obedience and rebellion over submission. This was only because his arrogance—by affirming an independent "I-ness" for himself—blinded his sight, so he found the matter of his own soul, which is fire, better than the matter of Adam's soul, which is clay. Thus, he judged that he was better than Adam, and that it is not fitting for the superior to humble himself in prostration to the inferior, even if Allah, the Glorified, commanded it. This is because he equated his own soul with the soul of his Lord through the independence and pride he saw for himself, thus leaving the Commander and clinging to the preponderating factors in the command.

In short, He, the Glorified, is Allah, from Whom everything begins and to Whom everything returns. If He creates something and judges it to have excellence, then it truly possesses excellence and honor in terms of external existence. If He creates a second thing and commands it to humble itself to the first, then its existence is deficient and inferior relative to that first thing. For the premise is that His command is either the very creative act of truth (نفس التكوين الحق) or leads to it. Thus, His word is the truth, and the obligation in complying with His command is to comply because it is His command, not because it contains a benefit (مصلحة) or an aspect of goodness and profit. Otherwise, He would be removed from His Lordship and Mastership, and the reins of command and influence would return to benefits and aspects—which themselves lead back to His creation and making, just like all other things without difference.

The sum of what the verses of the story indicate is that Iblis only disobeyed and deserved expulsion by being arrogant against Allah in failing to comply with His command, and that what he manifested his arrogance with was his saying: "I am better than him." In this, he was arrogant against his Lord, as previously explained, even if it was also arrogance against Adam in that he preferred himself over him and belittled his status. Allah had specifically honored Adam and informed them that he was more noble than them in the discourse on vicegerency and in His saying: "and I breathed into him of My spirit" and His saying: "I created with My [own] two hands." However, the focus in the verses is on his arrogance against Allah, not his arrogance against Adam.

Evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ كَانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ — **"When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis. He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed the command of his Lord"** (18:50), where He did not say: "so he disdained to humble himself to Adam," but rather mentioned the transgression (الفسق) against the command of the Lord, the Exalted.

In summary, the verses of the story focus on the issue of his seeking superiority over his Lord. As for his arrogance against Adam and the argument he used for it, that is understood by implication. This is apparently the secret behind not explicitly addressing the refutation of his argument, except for what is hinted at by some parts of the speech, such as His saying: "I created with My [own] two hands" and His saying: "and I breathed into him of My spirit," and so on.

**If you say:** To say that the command to prostrate was a creative command (تكوينيا) contradicts what the verses explicitly state regarding the disobedience of Iblis. For that which is capable of disobedience and opposition is only the legislative command (الأمر التشريعي); as for the creative command, it absolutely does not accept disobedience or rebellion, for it is the word of bringing into being (الإيجاد) from which existence never fails to follow. He said: إِنَّمَا قَوْلُنَا لِشَيْءٍ إِذَا أَرَدْنَاهُ أَنْ نَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — **"All that We say to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is"** (16:40).

**I say:** What we mentioned just now is that the story—with what it contains in its form of command, compliance, rebellion, expulsion, and so on—although it resembles the social matters familiar among us, it narrates a creative process (جريان تكويني) in the real relationships that exist between man, the angels, and Iblis. It actually clarifies the nature of the creation of the angels and Iblis as they relate to man, and what the natures of the two groups necessitate regarding the felicity and wretchedness of man. This is different from the command being creative in the literal sense.

The story is a creative one represented in a form we are familiar with from the forms of our social worldly life—like a king among kings who turns toward one of his common subjects because he perceives in him perfect readiness and complete aptitude. He chooses him for himself, honors him with special care, and makes him his vicegerent in his kingdom, preferring him over his elite circle around him. He commands them to humble themselves to his station and to work before him. A group of them responds to his call and complies with his command; he is pleased with them for that and confirms them in their positions. But some of them act arrogantly and judge the king to be in error in his command, so they do not comply, arguing that they are more noble in essence and more abundant in action. He becomes angry with them, expels them from his presence, and strikes them with humiliation and belittlement. For a king is only obeyed as a king because the reins of command are in his hand and from him issue decrees and regulations; he is not obeyed because what he commanded corresponds to an actual benefit—for that is the role of the sincere advisor who guides to goodness and right conduct.

By reflecting on this example, you see that the king's elite—both the obedient and the disobedient—were equal before the issuance of the command in the rank of proximity, established at the level of service and the enclosure of honor, without any distinction between them. This remained until the command came to them from the Possessor of the Throne. At that point, the path branched into two, and they split into two groups: an obedient, complying group, and a disobedient, arrogant group. Through this, the king's latent traits, the aspects of his power, and the forms of his will—of mercy, wrath, bringing near, distancing, pardon, forgiveness, seizing, vengeance, promise, threat, reward, and punishment—became manifest. Events are like a touchstone; through its friction, the essence of the metal reveals the quality or defect it possesses.

The story of the angels' prostration and the refusal of Iblis points to truths that resemble, in some way, the truths contained in this example. The command to prostrate therein is His honoring, the Exalted, of Adam with proximity of rank, the blessing of vicegerency, and the honor of guardianship (الولاية)—an honoring to which the angels humbled themselves and from which Iblis was distanced due to the opposition of the essence of human felicity. Thus, he began to corrupt the matter for man whenever he touched him and to pervert him if he drew near to him; it was written of him that whoever takes him as a guardian, he will surely lead him astray.

Allah, the Glorified, has expressed the execution of His creative command in several places in His speech using the word "command" or its like, such as His saying: فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَا أَتَيْنَا طَائِعِينَ — **"and said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly’"** (41:11), and His saying: إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَنْ يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا — **"Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, but they refused to undertake it and were apprehensive of it"** (33:72). Most comprehensive of all is His saying: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — **"All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is"** (36:82).

**If you say:** Letting go of the apparent meaning of the story and interpreting it as purely creative necessitates ambiguity in all of His speech. There would then be nothing to prevent interpreting the knowledge of the Beginning and the Return (المبدأ والمعاد), and even the stories, lessons, and laws, as parables. Permitting this would be an invalidation of the religion.

**I say:** What is to be followed is the evidence (الدليل). Sometimes evidence points to their literal reality and their explicit, definitive nature—such as the fundamental knowledge, the true beliefs, the stories of the prophets and nations in their religious calls, the laws and rulings, and the subsequent reward and punishment, and the like. At other times, evidence points and indications stand to the contrary, as in the story we are currently discussing, and like the story of the *Dharr* (the primordial covenant), the presentation of the Trust, and other matters whose denial does not entail the rejection of any of the necessities of the religion, nor does it contradict a decisive verse (آية محكمة), an established Sunnah, or a certain proof.

What Iblis mentioned in the context of argumentation: "I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay," is an analogy (القياس)—a speculative argument (استدلال ظني) that carries no weight in the market of realities. The exegetes have mentioned many ways to refute him, but you have known that the Quran paid no heed to his argument. Allah only took Iblis to task for his arrogance against Him in a station where he had nothing but submission and abasement. For this reason, we have refrained from addressing what they mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ فَاهْبِطْ مِنْهَا فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَنْ تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا فَاخْرُجْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ — **"He said, ‘Get down from it! It is not for you to be arrogant here. So begone! You are indeed among the degraded.’"** (7:13). Arrogance (التكبر) is when a person takes greatness (الكبر) for himself, for example, and manifests it over others. Greatness and smallness are relative matters, and they are mostly used in conceptual meanings. If a person manifests through word or deed that he is greater than another in honor, status, or the like, he has acted arrogantly toward him and considered him small. Since there is no honor or dignity for anything over another except what Allah has honored and dignified, arrogance is an absolutely blameworthy attribute in anyone other than the Exalted. For everything other than the Exalted possesses nothing but poverty and abasement in themselves, without difference between one thing and another; there is no dignity except by Allah and from Him. Thus, it is not for anyone besides Allah to be arrogant toward anyone. Rather, it is a specific attribute of Allah, the Glorified; He is the Great (الكبير), the Exalted (المتعال) absolutely. Some arrogance is true and praised—which is that which belongs to Allah, exalted is His Name, or leads back to Him in some way, such as arrogance toward the enemies of Allah, which is in reality seeking might through Allah. Some of it is false and blameworthy—which is that which exists in others by claiming greatness for themselves, not by right.

"The degraded (الصاغرين)" is the plural of *saghir* (صاغر), from *saghar* (صغار), which is ignominy and abasement. Degradation in conceptual meanings is like smallness in forms. His saying: فَاخْرُجْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ — **"So begone! You are indeed among the degraded"** — is an explanation and emphasis of His saying: فَاهْبِطْ مِنْهَا — **"Get down from it!"** because "getting down" (الهبوط) is the exit of a thing from its place of establishment while descending. This indicates that the mentioned descent was a conceptual descent (هبوطا معنويا), not a descent from one physical place to another. This supports what was mentioned earlier—that the referent of the pronoun in His saying "from it (مِنْهَا)" and "in it (فِيهَا)" is the rank (المنزلة), rather than the heaven or the Garden, unless they refer back to the rank in some way.

The meaning is: Allah, the Exalted, said: Descend from your rank since you did not prostrate when I commanded you. For this rank is a rank of abasement and submission to Me, so it is not right for you to be arrogant within it. So begone! You are indeed among the degraded—the people of ignominy. He was seized with degradation to counter his arrogance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ أَنْظِرْنِي إِلَى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ قَالَ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ — **"He said, ‘Respite me until the day they are resurrected.’ He said, ‘You are indeed among the respited.’"** (7:14-15). This is a request for respite and the granting of it. Allah, the Exalted, detailed this in another place, saying: قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنْظِرْنِي إِلَى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ — **"He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me until the day they are resurrected.’ He said, ‘You are indeed among the respited until the day of the known time.’"** (15:36-38, 38:79-81). From this, it is known that he was respited with a restriction, not with the absolute respite he mentioned. He was not respited until the Day of Resurrection; rather, Allah set a term for his respite before that, which is "the day of the known time." The discussion regarding this will come in *Surah al-Hijr*, if Allah wills.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ — **"You are indeed among the respited"** — only indicates the summary of what he was respited with. It also contains an indication that there are others respited besides him.

His request for respite until the Day of Resurrection indicates that his aim was to continue perverting this species in the world and in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) together until the Resurrection occurs. Allah, the Glorified, did not grant him what he requested; rather, He perhaps granted him that until the end of the world, excluding the intermediate realm. Thus, he has no authority in the intermediate realm—the authority of perversion and whispering—even if he might accompany man after his death in the intermediate realm as a spouse or companion (قرين). This is indicated by the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمَنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطَانًا فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ حَتَّى إِذَا جَاءَنَا قَالَ يَا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ وَلَنْ يَنْفَعَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ إِذْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِي الْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ — **"We assign a devil to be the companion of him who turns away from the remembrance of the All-beneficent. Indeed they bar them from the way, while they think they are rightly guided. When he comes to Us, he will say, ‘I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion!’ [It will be said to them,] ‘It will not benefit you today, as you were unjust, that you are partners in the punishment.’"** (43:36-39), and the apparent meaning of His saying: احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَأَزْوَاجَهُمْ — **"[The angels will be told,] ‘Muster the wrongdoers and their mates’"** (37:22).

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ فَبِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَاطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ ثُمَّ لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ — **"He said, ‘As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path. Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear’"** (7:16-17) until the end of the verse. Perversion (الإغواء) is to cast into error (الغي). Error and perversity (الغواية) refer to misguidance in some way, destruction, and failure. The phrase—namely His saying: أَغْوَيْتَنِي — although interpreted with each of these meanings according to the differing views of the exegetes, His saying, the Exalted, in *Surah al-Hijr* narrating from him: قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — **"He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them, all together’"** (15:39) supports that his intent is the first meaning. The *ba* in His saying: فَبِمَا is for causality (السببية) or recompense (المقابلة). The meaning is: "Because of Your perverting me" or "In return for Your perverting me, I will surely lie in wait for them..." etc. He who said it is for an oath (القسم) was mistaken; perhaps the speaker wanted to align it with His saying, the Exalted, in another place narrating from him: قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — **"He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them, all together’"** (38:82).

His saying: لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَاطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ — **"I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path"** — means: I will surely sit for their sake on Your straight path and Your even way that leads them to You and ends with them at their felicity. This is because everyone is traveling toward You, traversing—of necessity—Your straight path. Sitting on the straight path is a metaphor for adhering to it and lying in wait for those passing through it to lead them out of it.

His saying: ثُمَّ لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَعَنْ أَيْمَانِهِمْ وَعَنْ شَمَائِلِهِمْ — **"Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear, and from their right and from their left"** — is a clarification of what he will do to them while he lies in wait for them, sitting on the straight path. It is that he comes at them from every one of their four sides.

Since the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — upon which he lies in wait is a conceptual matter, the directions from which he comes at them are conceptual, not sensory. What is familiar from His speech, the Exalted, to identify the intent of these directions—such as His saying: يَعِدُهُمْ وَيُمَنِّيهِمْ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا — **"He makes them promises and gives them [false] hopes, and the Devil does not promise them anything but deceit"** (4:120), and His saying: إِنَّمَا ذَلِكُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِيَاءَهُ — **"It is only the Devil who frightens his allies"** (3:175), and His saying: وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُوَاتِ الشَّيْطَانِ — **"and do not follow in the footsteps of Satan"** (2:168), and His saying: الشَّيْطَانُ يَعِدُكُمُ الْفَقْرَ وَيَأْمُرُكُمْ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ — **"The Devil threatens you with poverty and bids you to indecency"** (2:268), among many other verses—is that the intent of "from their front" is what faces them of events during their lives, involving hopes and wishes regarding things the souls desire and natures find pleasurable, and things man dislikes and fears will befall him, such as poverty which he fears if he spends wealth in the way of Allah, or the blame and reproach of people if he enters one of the paths of goodness and reward.

The intent of "from their rear" is the side of children and descendants. For man has hopes, wishes, fears, and dislikes regarding those he leaves behind of his children. It is imagined to him that he remains through their remaining; thus, what pleases them pleases him and what grieves them grieves him. So he gathers wealth from its lawful and unlawful sources for their sake, and prepares for them whatever strength he can, thereby destroying himself for the sake of their lives.

The intent of "the right (اليمين)" — which is the strong and auspicious side of man — is the side of their felicity, which is religion. His coming from the right side is to glamorize for them exaggeration in some religious matters, and to burden themselves with what Allah has not commanded them—which is what Allah, the Exalted, calls "following the footsteps of Satan."

The intent of "the left (الشمال)" is the opposite of the right. His coming from it is to glamorize for them indecency and wrong, and to call them to commit disobediences, perpetrate sins, and follow whims.

Al-Zamakhshari said in *al-Kashshaf*: "If you say: Why was it said: 'from (مِنْ) their front and from (مِنْ) their rear' with the particle of beginning, and 'from (عَنْ) their right and from (عَنْ) their left' with the particle of passing over? I say: The adverbial object (المفعول فيه) has the verb transitive to it in the same way it is transitive to the direct object. Just as the particles of transitivity varied in that, they varied in this; it was a language to be taken [as heard] and not measured by analogy. One only investigates the correctness of its placement. When we heard them saying: 'He sat *'an* (عَنْ) his right' and '*'ala* (عَلَى) his right,' and 'He sat *'an* (عَنْ) his left' and '*'ala* (عَلَى) his left,' we said: The meaning of '*'ala* his right' is that he established himself on the right side with the establishment of the one who is above over the one who is below. The meaning of '*'an* his right' is that he sat apart from the person on the right, inclined away from him and not touching him. Then it became frequent until it was used for both the one apart and otherwise, as we mentioned regarding 'come (تعال)'." (End of the relevant portion).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شَاكِرِينَ — **"and You will not find most of them to be grateful"** (7:17) — is the result of what he mentioned of his action toward them in his saying: "I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path, then I will surely come at them..." etc. In what Allah narrated of his speech in another place, he placed another phrase instead of this one, namely "and You will not find most of them to be grateful." He said: قَالَ أَرَأَيْتَكَ هَذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا — **"He said, ‘Tell me, this one whom You have honored above me, if You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, [all] but a few.’"** (17:62). Thus, he excepted a few from his whispering and perversion, corresponding to what is in this Surah. He also said: لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — **"I will surely pervert them, all together, except Your devoted servants among them."** (15:39-40, 38:82-83).

From this, it appears that he meant by "the grateful (الشاكرين)" in this place "the devoted (المخلصين)." Careful reflection on the meaning of the two words guides to this. For the devoted (المخلصين) — with the *fathah* on the *lam* — are those whom Allah has purified for Himself; thus, no one shares with Him in them—that is, in their servitude and worship. No one else has a portion in them. They do not remember anyone but their Lord, having forgotten everything besides Him, even themselves. Thus, there is nothing in their hearts but He, the Glorified; there is no place therein for Satan or for his glamorizations.

The grateful (الشاكرون) are those in whom the attribute of gratitude has become established absolutely. They do not touch a blessing except with gratitude—that is, by using it and disposing of it, in word or deed, in a manner that manifests that it is from their Lord, the Bestower of blessings upon them. They do not turn toward anything—whether themselves or others—except that they are in remembrance of their Lord before they touch it, with it, and after it, and that it is His property, the Exalted, entirely, having no command over anything. Their remembrance of their Lord in this manner makes them forget the remembrance of others except through Allah. And Allah has not made for any man two hearts in his breast.

If the word were given the full right of its meaning, the grateful would be the devoted. Iblis's exception of the grateful or the devoted from the scope of his perversion and misguidance was done by him out of necessity, based on the reality of the matter; he did not bring it forth randomly, or as a favor to the children of Adam, or out of mercy, or for any other reason.

This is what Iblis faced the Source of Might and Greatness with—namely his saying: "As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path"—until his saying—"and You will not find most of them to be grateful." Thus, he informed that he targets them from every possible direction and corrupts the matter for most of them by leading them out of the straight path. He did not clarify the manner of his act or the quality of his work.

However, in his speech, there is an indication of two truths: One of them is that the perversity (الغواية) that became established in his soul—which he attributes to the act of Allah—is the cause for his misguiding and perverting them. That is, he touches them with his own perverse soul, so he deposits nothing in them but perversity, like fire that touches water with its heat and thus heats it. This truth is manifest from His saying, the Exalted: احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَأَزْوَاجَهُمْ — until He said — وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَى بَعْضٍ يَتَسَاءَلُونَ قَالُوا إِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَأْتُونَنا عَنِ الْيَمِينِ قَالُوا بَلْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ — until He said — فَأَغْوَيْنَاكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غَاوِينَ — **"[The angels will be told,] ‘Muster the wrongdoers and their mates’... and they will turn to one another, questioning each other. They will say, ‘Indeed you used to come to us from the right.’ They will say, ‘No, you [yourselves] were not believers’... So we perverted you, for we were ourselves perverse.’"** (37:22-32).

The second: That which Satan touches of the children of Adam—which is the nature of his act and work—is the human consciousness and his vital thinking related to the conceptualization of things and the affirmation of what ought to be done or ought not to be done. Its detail will come in the discussion regarding Iblis and his work.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ اخْرُجْ مِنْهَا مَذْءُومًا مَدْحُورًا لَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ — **"He said, ‘Get out of it, blamed and banished! Whoever of them follows you’"** (7:18) etc. "Blamed (المذءوم)" is from *dhama* (ذامه), meaning he found fault with him and censured him. "Banished (المدحور)" is from *dahara* (دحره), meaning he expelled him and pushed him away with ignominy.

His saying: لَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ — **"Whoever of them follows you"** etc. The *lam* is for an oath, and its response is His saying: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ — **"I will surely fill hell"** etc. Since the subject of Iblis's speech—which was in the form of a threat of vengeance—was the children of Adam, and that he would invalidate the purpose of creation in them, which is their being grateful, the Exalted answered him with what He would do to them and to him. He said: لَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ — **"Whoever of them follows you"** — in parallel to his speech, then He said: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — **"I will surely fill hell with you all"** — meaning from you and from them. Thus, He made him a partner in the recompense with them.

The Exalted showed favor in this word of His, which must be fulfilled, for He did not mention all who followed him; rather, He brought His saying: مِنْكُمْ — **"with you [all]"** — which implies partitive meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَيَا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ أَنْتَ وَزَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ — **"‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in the Garden’"** (7:19) until the end of the verse. He specified Adam (peace be upon him) with the address and joined his wife with him in the ruling. His saying: فَكُلَا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا — **"and eat from wherever you wish"** — is an expansion in the permission of disposal, except for what He excepted with His saying: وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ — **"but do not approach this tree."** The wrongdoing (الظلم) is wrongdoing against the self, rather than disobedience to a legislative command, for the command was advisory (إرشادي).

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا الشَّيْطَانُ — **"Then the Devil whispered to them"** (7:20) until the end of the verse. Whispering (الوسوسة) is to call toward a matter with a hidden voice. Concealment (المواراة) is to hide a thing by placing it behind what covers it. "Nakedness (السوآة)" is the plural of *saw'ah* (سوأة), which is the limb that it grieves a person to manifest and uncover. His saying: مَا نَهَاكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ — **"Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels"** etc., means: except out of dislike that you should become angels or become among the immortal.

"Angel (الملك)", although read with a *fathah* on the *lam*, contains the meaning of "sovereignty (المُلك)" — with a *dammah* and then *sukun*. The evidence for this is His saying in another place: قَالَ يَا آدَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَى شَجَرَةِ الْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَا يَبْلَى — **"He said, ‘O Adam! Shall I guide you to the tree of immortality and a kingdom that never ages?’"** (20:120).

It is narrated in *al-Majma'*, from al-Sayyid al-Murtada (may Allah have mercy on him), the possibility that the intent of His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ — **"lest you should become angels"** etc., is that he deluded them into thinking that the prohibition from consuming the tree was specifically for the angels and the immortals, excluding the two of them. It would be like one of us saying to another: "You were not forbidden from such-and-such except that you should be so-and-so," meaning that the one forbidden is so-and-so, not you. This would be more emphatic in creating doubt and confusion for them. (End of quote). However, the verse of *Surah Taha* quoted just now refutes this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَقَاسَمَهُمَا إِنِّي لَكُمَا لَمِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ — **"And he swore to them, ‘I am indeed one of your well-wishers.’"** (7:21). Swearing (المقاسمة) is exaggeration in the oath; that is, he swore to them and was emphatic in his oath that he was indeed one of their well-wishers. Sincere advice (النصح) is the opposite of deception (الغش).

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَدَلَّاهُمَا بِغُرُورٍ — **"Thus he misled them by delusion"** (7:22) until the end of the verse. Leading (التدلية) is to bring near and to cause to reach, just as "dangling (التدلي)" is to draw near and let down. It is as if it is a metaphor from "I let down the bucket (دلوت الدلو)," meaning I sent it down. Delusion (الغرور) is to manifest sincere advice while concealing deception. "Stitching (الخصف)" is to join and gather; from it is "stitching the sandal."

In His saying: وَنَادَاهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَنْ تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ — **"And their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree?’"** — there is an indication that when this address was directed to them, they were in a state of distance from their Lord, because "calling out (النداء)" is to call from afar. Likewise, they were far from the tree, as evidenced by His saying: تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ — **"that tree"** — in contrast to His saying when they first entered the Garden: وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ — **"do not approach this tree."**

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَا رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنْفُسَنَا وَإِنْ لَمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ — **"They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers.’"** (7:23). This is from them the ultimate degree of abasement and supplication. For this reason, they did not ask for anything; they only mentioned their need for forgiveness and mercy, and the threat of permanent, absolute loss for them, until Allah wills what He wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ — **"He said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another!’"** (7:24) until the end of the verse. The address was to Adam, his wife, and Iblis. The enmity of some toward others is what is witnessed of the difference in their natures. This is a decree (قضاء) from the Exalted. The other decree is His saying: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَى حِينٍ — **"On the earth shall be your abode and a provision for a while"** — meaning until the end of the worldly life. The apparent context is that the second address is also shared by the three.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ — **"He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth.’"** (7:25). This is another decree necessitating their attachment to the earth until the time of Resurrection. It is not unlikely that this address is specific to Adam, his wife, and their children, due to the separation with the word "He said (قَالَ)." The detailed discussion regarding the story of the Garden has already passed in *Surah al-Baqarah*, so let whoever wishes refer to it.

## A Discourse on Iblis and His Work

The subject of "Iblis" has become a commonplace topic among us, one to which little heed is paid beyond our occasional mention of him to curse him, seek refuge in Allah from him, or disparage some of our thoughts as being "satanic" suggestions, whisperings, or incitements. We do this without reflecting so as to grasp what the Noble Quran provides regarding the reality of this wondrous being—who is hidden from our senses—and his extraordinary influence and authority (الولاية) within the human world.

And how could it be otherwise, when he accompanies the human world across its vast and wondrous scope from the moment it appeared in existence until its term is completed and it becomes extinct with the folding up of the carpet of this world? He then remains attached to man after death, becoming his close companion (القرين) until he leads him into the Eternal Fire. He is with each one of us just as he is with others; he is with him in his public life and his private secrets, running with him wherever he runs, even in the most hidden imagination he conceives in a corner of his mind, or a thought he hides in the folds of his innermost soul (السريرة). No veil hides man from him, nor does any distracting preoccupation divert him.

As for the researchers among us, they have neglected the investigation of this matter, building upon what the early researchers established and following the path of inquiry they laid out. These are the naive theories that first occur to common understandings upon receiving the Divine Speech, followed by disputation over what the understanding of each particular sect is guided toward, entrenching themselves within it and defending it through various forms of polemics (الجدال). They occupy themselves with enumerating the difficulties of the story and setting forth question and answer, one after another:

Why did Allah create Iblis when He knew who he was? Why did He include him among the assembly of the angels when he was not of them? Why did He command him to prostrate when He knew he would not obey? Why did He not grant him the success (التوفيق) to prostrate and instead led him astray (أغواه)? Why did He not destroy him when he failed to prostrate? Why did He grant him respite until the Day they are resurrected, or until the Day of the Known Time? Why did He empower him over the children of Adam with this wondrous empowerment by which he flows through them like the circulation of blood? Why did He support him with hosts of cavalry and infantry and give him mastery over everything that touches human life? Why did He not make him manifest to human senses so that man might guard against his touch? Why did He not support man with the like of what He supported him with? Why did He not conceal the secrets of the creation of Adam and his children from Iblis so that he would not aspire to lead them astray? And how was direct address (المشافهة) permissible between him and Allah, the Glorified, when he is the furthest of creation from Him and the most hated by Him, and he was neither a prophet nor an angel? It was said: by way of a miracle; and it was said: by the creation of effects that indicate the intended meaning—yet there is no evidence for any of that.

Furthermore, how did Iblis enter Paradise? How was it permissible for whispering (الوسوسة), lying, and disobedience (المعصية) to occur there, when it is a place of purity and sanctity? How did Adam believe him when his word contradicted the report of Allah? How did he aspire to kingdom and immortality when that contradicts the belief in the Return (المعاد)? How was disobedience permissible for him when he was an infallible prophet? How was his repentance accepted yet he was not returned to his initial station, while the one who repents from sin is like one who has no sin? And how...? And how...?

The neglect of researchers in pursuing true inquiry and their indulgence in polemical objection and response reached such a point that some suggested that what is meant by "Adam" is the "generic Adam" (آدم النوعي) and that the story is purely symbolic (تخييلية). Others chose the view that the Iblis reported by the Noble Quran is merely the force within man that calls toward evil!

Others went so far as to permit the attribution of foul and loathsome acts to Allah, claiming that all acts of disobedience are His doing, and that He creates evil and the loathsome, thus spoiling what He rectifies; and that "good" (الحسن) is simply what He commanded and "evil" (القبيح) is what He forbade. Others claimed that Adam was not a prophet; others that prophets are not infallible (معصومين) at all; others that they are not infallible before their mission, and the story of Paradise occurred before Adam's mission; and others that all of this was a trial (امتحان) and a test—yet they did not explain the true criterion (الملاك) in this trial by which many are led astray and the majority perish. Were it not for the existence of a criterion that severs the root of the problem, all these difficulties would return.

That which prevents the success of effort in these inquiries and disrupts their results is that they did not distinguish, in these discussions, between their ontological aspects (الجهات الحقيقية) and their conventional aspects (الجهات الاعتبارية). They did not separate the creative order (التكوين) from the legislative order (التشريع), and thus the system of inquiry became disordered. They frequently applied the conventional, man-made principles (الأصول الوضيعة الاعتبارية) that govern legislative and social matters to the realm of the creative order.

What must be clarified and refined by the free researcher of these religious truths—which are linked to the aspects of the creative order—is to clarify several points:

**First:** That the existence of any thing among the things to which creation and origination (الإيجاد) pertain is, in itself—meaning its essential existence (وجوده النفسي) without relation to anything else—nothing but good (خير) and occurs only as beautiful (حسن). If we were to assume, per impossibile, that creation pertained to something assumed to be evil in itself, it would still be an existing thing with existential effects, originating from Allah, sustained by His provision, and ultimately returning to Him. Its state would be like that of all other creation, containing no trace of evil or foulness, unless its existence is related to another thing such that it corrupts a just order in existence or necessitates the deprivation of a group of beings from their good and happiness. This is the aforementioned "relation" (الإضافة).

Therefore, it was necessary in Divine Wisdom (الحكمة الإلهية) that the benefit derived from these existentially harmful things should outweigh their harm. This is His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ** — "who perfected everything He created" (32:7); and His saying: **تَبارَكَ اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ** — "Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (7:54); and His saying: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ** — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification" (18:44).

**Second:** That the world of making and origination, despite the multiplicity of its parts and the vastness of its breadth, is interconnected, its end folded back to its beginning. The origination of a part is only through the origination of the whole, and the rectification of the part is only through the rectification of the whole. The difference existing between the parts of the world in existence—which is what made the world a "world"—and their subsequent interconnection, necessitates by a necessary entailment in Divine Wisdom the relation of some to others by way of incompatibility and opposition (التضاد), or by perfection and deficiency, possession and loss, attainment and deprivation. Were it not for this, all things would return to a single thing in which there is no distinction or difference, and existence would thereby be nullified. The Exalted said: **وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ** — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye" (54:50).

Thus, were it not for evil, corruption, toil, loss, deficiency, weakness, and the like in this world, there would be no instance (مصداق) for good, health, ease, possession, perfection, and strength; nor would any meaning be conceived of them, for we only derive meanings from their instances.

Were it not for wretchedness (الشقاء), there would be no happiness (السعادة); were it not for disobedience (المعصية), obedience (الطاعة) would not be realized; were it not for foulness and blame, there would be no beauty or praise; were it not for punishment, no reward would be obtained; and were it not for this world (الدنيا), the Hereafter (الآخرة) would not be formed.

Obedience, for example, is the compliance with a masterly command. If the absence of compliance—which is disobedience—were impossible, the act would be necessary and inevitable. With the necessity of the act, there is no meaning to a masterly command, as it is impossible to seek what is already attained (تحصيل الحاصل). Without a masterly command, there is no instance for obedience nor any concept for it, as you have seen.

With the nullification of obedience and disobedience, praise and blame related to them are nullified, as are reward and punishment, promise and threat, warning and glad tidings, then religion (الدين), the law (الشريعة), and the call (الدعوة), then prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة), then society and civilization, then humanity, then everything. By this analogy, all opposing matters in the system are understood. So understand this.

From here, it becomes clear to you that the existence of the Devil (الشيطان) who calls toward evil and disobedience is one of the pillars of the system of the human world, which only proceeds according to the sunnah of choice (الاختيار) and aims for the happiness of the species.

He is like the margin (الحاشية) surrounding the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — which it is in the nature of this species to traverse, toiling toward its Lord to meet Him. It is well known that a path is only defined as a path by its main body through the margin that lies outside of it and borders it. Were it not for the edge, there would be no middle. So understand this, and remember His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ فَبِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِراطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** — "He said, ‘As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path’" (7:16); and His saying: **قالَ : هذا صِراطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ** — "He said, ‘This is a straight path [leading] to Me. Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you’" (15:41-42).

If you reflect upon these two aspects and then contemplate the verses of the story of prostration, you will find them to be an image informing of the real ontological relations (الروابط الواقعية) between the human species, the angels, and Iblis. These relations are expressed through command, compliance, arrogance, expulsion, stoning, and question and answer. All the difficulties raised regarding it arise from a deficiency in contemplating the story. Even some who noticed the correct direction—that it refers to the natures of man, angel, and devil—mentioned that the command and prohibition (meaning the command to Iblis to prostrate and the prohibition to Adam from eating from the tree) were creative (تكوينيان). By this, they corrupted what they had rectified, for they overlooked that creative commands and prohibitions do not accept violation or opposition, yet Iblis opposed the command and Adam violated the prohibition.

**Third:** That the story of Paradise—its meaning, as detailed previously in Surah al-Baqarah—informs us that Allah, the Glorified, created a celestial, intermediate Paradise (جنة برزخية سماوية) and placed Adam in it before earthly life was established for him and before masterly obligation (التكليف المولوي) covered him. This was to test the human nature (الطباع الإنساني) thereby, so it would become manifest that man cannot but live on the earth and be raised in the lap of command and prohibition, thus deserving happiness and Paradise through obedience; and if he is less than that, then he receives less. Man cannot stand in the station of proximity or alight in the abode of happiness except by traversing this path.

By this, it is revealed that none of the difficulties they raised regarding the story of Paradise remain. For Paradise was not the Paradise of Eternity (جنة الخلد) which none enter except a friend (ولي) among the friends of Allah—an entry after which there is never an exit. Nor was the abode an earthly abode in which one lives an earthly life managed by legislation and governed by masterly command and prohibition. Rather, it was an abode in which the judgment of the human disposition (السجية الإنسانية) was manifested—not the disposition of Adam (peace be upon him) as the person of Adam, for he was not commanded to prostrate nor entered into Paradise except because he was "man," as previously explained.

(1) The author of *al-Manar* in Volume 8 of the *Tafsir* under the heading "Difficulties in the Story."

Returning to the beginning of the discourse:

Allah, the Glorified, did not describe the essence of this evil creature whom He named Iblis except briefly, which is His saying, the Exalted: **كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ** — "He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed against his Lord’s command" (18:50); and what He narrated of his words: **خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ** — "You created me from fire." Thus, He clarified that the beginning of his creation was from fire, of the genus of the jinn. As for what his state became ultimately, He did not mention it explicitly, just as He did not mention the details of his creation as He detailed the creation of man.

Yes, there are verses describing his craft and work from which what is beneficial in this chapter can be derived. The Exalted said, narrating from him: **لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِراطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ ثُمَّ لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَعَنْ أَيْمانِهِمْ وَعَنْ شَمائِلِهِمْ وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ** — "‘I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path. Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear, and from their right and their left, and You will not find most of them to be grateful’" (7:16-17).

Thus, he informed that he influences them from the direction of psychological emotions—fear, hope, desire, longing, lust, and anger—and then in their thoughts and wills that arise from them.

This is paralleled in meaning by His saying: **قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth’" (15:39). That is: I will surely beautify for them false, vile, and hideous matters with ornaments and decorations prepared from the attachment of emotions that call toward following them. I will lead them astray thereby—as with adultery, for example: man conceives it, lust beautifies it in his sight, and by its power, whatever occurs to his mind of the danger in committing it weakens; thus he accepts it and commits it. Similar to this is His saying: **يَعِدُهُمْ وَيُمَنِّيهِمْ وَما يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً** — "He makes them promises and gives them [false] hopes, and the Devil does not promise them anything but deceit" (4:120); and His saying: **فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — "the Devil glamorized their deeds for them" (16:63).

All of this—as you see—indicates that the field of his action is human perception (الإدراك الإنساني), and the means of his action are the internal emotions and sensations. He is the one who casts these false delusions and vain thoughts into the human soul, as indicated by His saying: **الْوَسْواسِ الْخَنَّاسِ الَّذِي يُوَسْوِسُ فِي صُدُورِ النَّاسِ** — "the sneaking whisperer, who whispers into the breasts of mankind" (114:4-5).

However, man, despite this, does not doubt that these thoughts and delusions called "satanic whisperings" are thoughts of his own soul which he himself originates within it, without perceiving anyone else casting them to him or causing them in any way—just as in his other thoughts and opinions that do not relate to action, such as our saying: "One is half of two" or "Four is even," and the like.

Thus, it is man who originates these thoughts and delusions in himself, just as it is the Devil who casts them to him and brings them to his mind without any mutual interference (تزاحم). If his causing them were like the causalities current among us—where someone conveys a report or a judgment to us—his casting them to us would not be compatible with our independence in thinking. The attribution of the voluntary act (الفعل الاختياري) to us would be nullified because the knowledge, preference, and will would belong to him, not to us, and no blame or disparagement would follow the act.
